html:
Satan told Allah he would mislead humans — and Allah agreed Strange / Obscure Moderate Q 15:39-40
"He [Iblis] said: 'My Lord, because You have put me in error, I will surely make [disobedience] attractive to them on earth, and I will mislead them all — except Your chosen servants from them.'"

What the verses say

Satan requests — and receives — divine permission to mislead humans. The arrangement is explicit: Allah authorizes Satan's mission to beautify disobedience for all but the chosen.

Why this is a problem

A theology where Allah explicitly authorizes an adversary to mislead humans — and then judges humans for being misled — has a fairness problem built into its design. Classical compatibilism (khalq/kasb) attempts to patch this by dividing divine creation of acts from human acquisition of them, but the patch concedes the game is structurally weighted against ordinary believers from the start.

Why it fails

Divinely authorized tempter plus divine judgment for failing the test is the theodicy problem the verse makes explicit. "Operating within limits" does not address the structural asymmetry; it restates it. Classical compatibilism concedes the asymmetry without eliminating it.

Tawaf and stone-kissing — pre-Islamic ritual preserved Strange / Obscure Moderate Q 22:29
"Let them complete their prescribed duties, fulfill their vows, and circumambulate the Ancient House."

What the verses say

Tawaf — counterclockwise circling of the Ka'ba — and stone-kissing are Quranic rituals that are directly continuous with pre-Islamic pagan practice. The idols were removed; the circumambulation was kept.

Why this is a problem

Pre-Islamic Arabs circumambulated the Ka'ba around its idols as a pagan rite. Islam removed the idols but retained the ritual form unchanged. Umar's famous declaration at the Black Stone — "I know you are just a stone" but "I saw the Prophet kiss you" — encapsulates the problem: the ritual survives on precedent, not on any coherent monotheistic rationale. A monotheism whose central pilgrimage preserves pagan circumambulation and stone-kissing while condemning other stone objects has not resolved its tension with what it replaced.

Why it fails

The Abrahamic founding narrative has no independent historical support. Retaining the form while changing the stated theology is rebadging, not abolition — and the hadith corpus preserves Umar explicitly acknowledging the stone's inherent meaninglessness while kissing it anyway.

"Marry slave girls with their families' permission" — slavery embedded in marriage law Women Moderate Q 4:25
"And whoever among you cannot afford to marry free, believing women, then [he may marry] from those whom your right hands possess of believing slave girls."

What the verse says

Marriage to believing slave girls is permitted when a man cannot afford free women. Free believing women are the first tier; slave women are the economic alternative.

Why this is a problem

Marriage is explicitly stratified by slave/free status, with slave women positioned as a budget option for men who cannot afford free wives. The requirement that a slave woman's owner must consent to the marriage locates ultimate authority over her life with her owner, not with herself. An eternal divine marriage code that carries "free" and "owned" as distinct moral-economic categories of women has embedded the 7th-century slave economy into permanent law.

Why it fails

If the verse intended abolition, it could have forbidden slavery — as the Quran forbids wine. It did not. The ranking (free women first, slave women as alternative) embeds the distinction into eternal law. "Elevation" that still requires an owner's consent for a woman's marriage is not emancipation.

Slave women receive half the punishment of free women Women Treatment of Disbelievers Moderate Q 4:25
"And if [slave women] commit immorality, their punishment is half that of free [unmarried] women."

What the verse says

The zina penalty for slave women is half that of free women — 50 lashes instead of 100. Divine justice is calibrated by social class.

Why this is a problem

The half-punishment framework encodes slave/free status directly into eternal divine law. The jurisprudential problem quickly becomes apparent: if the full penalty for a married woman is stoning, "half" cannot be applied — and classical jurists struggled with this incoherence. A legal system that calibrates punishment by ownership status has institutionalized that status as a permanent moral category.

Why it fails

"Mitigation" preserves the punishment framework and adjusts the slave's allocation within it. When the framework hits the stoning-cannot-be-halved problem, it reveals its own incoherence. A divine legal code calibrating penalty by slave/free status has made that status load-bearing in eternal law.

"Do not compel your slave girls to prostitution — if they desire chastity" Women Moderate Q 24:33
"Do not compel your slave girls to prostitution, if they desire chastity, to seek [thereby] the temporary interests of worldly life."

What the verse says

The rule: don't force slave girls into prostitution — if they desire chastity. The conditional phrase limits the protection to those who actively assert it.

Why this is a problem

The conditional "if they desire chastity" creates an obvious legal gap: if a slave girl does not explicitly claim chastity, the protection lapses. Classical commentators (Tabari, Ibn Kathir, al-Qurtubi) recognized and debated this implication — the fact that the question appears in tafsir confirms the conditional does real legal work. A scripture that issues a conditional prohibition on forced sexual service, rather than a categorical one, has done something other than ban the practice.

Why it fails

Arabic conditionals most naturally specify when the command applies. The "idiomatic" defense is philologically weak. If the intended meaning was categorical prohibition, the verse could simply say "do not compel your slave girls to prostitution" — the conditional would be omitted. Its presence is the difference between blanket prohibition and narrow-case rebuke.

Freeing slaves as atonement — slavery embedded in expiation Women Moderate Q 4:92
"Let him free a believing slave..." (accidental killing)
"Feed ten needy people or free a slave..." (broken oath)

What the verses say

Freeing a slave is prescribed as atonement for serious sins — accidental killing, broken oaths, and zihar divorce. Emancipation is a transaction for personal sin, not a moral principle.

Why this is a problem

The atonement economy presupposes the institution it claims to dissolve: you need slaves to free as expiation. If slavery were abolished, the entire mechanism collapses. Classical jurisprudence operated within this standing institution for 1,400 years and never treated Islamic law as requiring its abolition. "Trajectory toward abolition" is apologetic retrofit; the tradition never reached the endpoint because it never moved toward it as doctrinal requirement.

Why it fails

A mechanism that requires maintaining slavery to function cannot be described as designed to eliminate it. Removing slavery from the economy would remove the expiation mechanism. The atonement framework preserves the institution as structural background, not as a step toward its end.

Children of concubines — classical law's inheritance mechanics Women Moderate Classical tafsir on Q 4:24
[Classical law derived from Q 4:24:] "A concubine who bears her master's child (umm al-walad) cannot be sold; she is freed at his death."

What the law says

A female slave who bears her master's child gains special status: she becomes unsaleable and is freed upon his death. Before bearing a child, her status is full slavery.

Why this is a problem

The protection is triggered only by producing a male owner's child. A welfare system whose mechanism for eventual freedom is involuntary pregnancy has structured liberation around reproductive exploitation. The child becomes the key to the mother's freedom, tying her release to her use. Modern welfare frameworks would reject this design on first principles; classical Islamic law built it as divinely sanctioned protocol.

Why it fails

A welfare system that requires involuntary pregnancy as the trigger for eventual freedom has structured the institution around the owner's reproductive use of the slave. The child becomes the key to the mother's freedom, which ties her liberation to her exploitation — a design no contemporary welfare framework would endorse.

"Faces will be blackened on the Day of Resurrection" Treatment of Disbelievers Strange / Obscure Moderate Q 3:106
"On the Day [some] faces will turn white and [some] faces will turn black."

What the verses say

Judgment Day sorts the saved from the damned by face color: white for the saved, black for the condemned.

Why this is a problem

The white/black color symbolism for salvation/damnation maps directly onto racial terminology. Classical tafsir read the imagery as spiritual metaphor, but the metaphor chosen operates within a color hierarchy that has racial resonance impossible to ignore in modern application. When preachers deploy this imagery today, it carries racial valence regardless of intended theological content.

Why it fails

The Arabic idiom of honor/shame may be the intended frame, but eschatological imagery does not stay contained within intended frames once it enters a world where racial categories are operative. Classical tafsir itself mapped the imagery onto visible human distinctions, and modern preachers using it in multiracial contexts cannot fully neutralize the racial resonance by pointing to original intent.

"We have made it an Arabic Quran" — why would God prefer one language? Strange / Obscure Contradiction Moderate Q 12:2
"We have made it an Arabic Quran that you might understand." (12:2)
"Thus We have revealed to you an Arabic Quran that you may warn the Mother of Cities [Mecca]." (42:7)

What the verses say

The Quran explicitly notes its Arabic language — and Q 42:7 frames it as aimed primarily at warning Mecca. A universal revelation for all of humanity is justified by its local audience's language needs.

Why this is a problem

Non-Arabic speakers are structurally secondary: only Arabic recitation is liturgically valid in Islamic practice, meaning billions of Muslims recite prayers in a language they do not understand. Classical jurisprudence ruled that translations are not the Quran — only the Arabic is. A revelation that insists on its linguistic form as essential has privileged the original audience's ethnolinguistic group in a way that endures for all subsequent generations.

Why it fails

"Precision of Arabic" is an internal claim that cannot be verified against a divine original in another language. Requiring 1.8 billion people to worship in a language most do not speak is not universality; it is a linguistic gatekeeping that structurally advantages Arabic-speaking communities across fourteen centuries.

Muslim men may marry slave girls — with reduced obligations Women Treatment of Disbelievers Moderate Q 4:25
"And whoever among you cannot afford to marry free, believing women, then [he may marry] from believing slaves."

What the verse says

Marriage to slave girls is permitted as a lower-tier option for men who cannot afford free women. The reduced obligations that follow encode the slave woman's lesser status into divine marriage law.

Why this is a problem

The tiering of wives by slave/free status embeds ownership as a permanent moral-economic category in eternal divine law. Reduced obligations for the slave wife confirm her lower standing rather than equalizing it. A marriage system that ranks wives by whether they are owned property has commodified the female slave as an economic alternative.

Why it fails

Elevation within a stratified system is not the removal of stratification. Encoding "free woman first, slave woman as alternative" into eternal divine law means that whenever the verse is consulted, the hierarchy it embeds is consulted with it. A divine law for all time should not carry ownership status as a marriage variable.

Fight against your own kin — the Badr fracture Prophetic Character Moderate Q 58:22
"You will not find a people who believe in Allah and the Last Day having affection for those who oppose Allah and His Messenger, even if they were their fathers or their sons or their brothers or their kindred."

What the verse says

Muslims must not maintain affection for relatives who oppose Allah — even fathers, sons, or brothers. At Badr, this meant fighting and killing Meccan family members.

Why this is a problem

The verse's language is categorical and universal, not contextual: "you will not find a people who believe... and still love" opponents of Allah from among their own family. Classical tafsir applied this broadly — religious allegiance trumps family bonds when they conflict. Modern Muslim families facing apostate members still operate within this framework; ex-Muslims and converts report family-severance experiences grounded in this verse's logic.

Why it fails

The verse's categorical language — "even if they are their fathers or sons" — is permanent, not contextual. The "wartime only" reading is modern apologetic narrowing against the text's plain meaning and against how the classical tradition actually applied it.

Parable: the owned slave vs. the free man Slavery Moderate Q 16:75
"Allah presents an example: a slave [who is] owned and unable to do a thing and he to whom We have provided from Ourselves good provision, so he spends from it secretly and publicly. Can they be equal?"

What the verse says

God uses the self-evident inequality between slave and free as a rhetorical analogy for the inequality between idols and Allah. The argument only works if slavery's hierarchy is taken as a moral given.

Why this is a problem

Divine rhetoric that leans on "slave and free are obviously unequal" as a theological proof-point has ratified the institution it uses as scaffolding. A God opposed to slavery would not build a theological argument on the assumption that owned persons are self-evidently lesser. Choosing "owned slave, unable to do a thing" as the image for incapacity preserves the institution inside divine scripture as permanent moral vocabulary.

Why it fails

A rhetorical comparison whose force depends on the audience accepting slavery as an unquestioned backdrop is a comparison that ratifies the hierarchy. If the Quran had wanted to communicate without entrenching the category, it could have used other contrasts. The choice is not neutral; it makes the institution part of the divine vocabulary for all time.
